message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-OPS-1 (high): Lockfile-sync guard re-resolves against live PyPI (no --exclude-newer); spuriously fails
Location: .github/workflows/ci.yml ~126-146 (audit job "Lockfiles are in sync") + scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh
Claim: The gate runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes` and diffs vs the committed requirements.lock. With pyproject ranges and unconstrained transitive deps and NO --exclude-newer/resolution-date pin, uv resolves the NEWEST in-range versions at CI wall-clock time, so the gate goes red the instant any of ~218 transitive packages publishes an in-range release — independent of whether the PR touches deps. Claimed to be CURRENTLY failing on a clean checkout (e.g. mlflow 3.13.0→3.14.0). The reproducibility guard is itself irreproducible. VERIFY whether this actually re-resolves to newer (reproduce with the CI-pinned uv==0.11.21) and whether it blocks merges.

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-OPS-1. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify finding K-OPS-1 by examining the CI workflow and the lockfile regeneration script, then attempt to reproduce the claim.

content.content:
100	          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
101	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
102	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
103	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
104	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
105	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
106	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
107	
108	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
109	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
110	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
111	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
112	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
113	  audit:
114	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
115	    steps:
116	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
117	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
118	        with:
119	          python-version: "3.12"
120	      - name: Install pip-audit
121	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
122	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
123	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
124	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
125	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
126	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
127	        run: |
128	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
129	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
130	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
131	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
132	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
133	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
134	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
135	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
136	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
137	          # stay in sync too.
138	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
139	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
140	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
141	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
142	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
143	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
144	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
147	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
148	        run: |
149	          set -euo pipefail
150	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
151	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
152	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
153	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
154	          IGNORES=()
155	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
156	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
157	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
158	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
159	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
160	          fi
161	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
162	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
163	        run: |
164	          set -euo pipefail
165	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
166	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
167	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
168	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
169	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles from pyproject.toml (#G-016).
#
# Dependabot bumps the version ranges in pyproject.toml but cannot regenerate the uv lockfiles,
# so a Dependabot PR will fail the CI "requirements.lock is stale" gate until the lockfiles are
# regenerated. Run this on a Dependabot branch (or any PR that changes pyproject dependencies),
# then commit the updated requirements*.lock files:
#
#     scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh && git commit -am "chore: regenerate lockfiles"
#
# uv is pinned to the same version CI uses (#F-031) so the output matches the sync check exactly.
set -euo pipefail

UV_VERSION="0.11.21"

if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1 || [ "$(uv --version | awk '{print $2}')" != "$UV_VERSION" ]; then
  echo "installing uv==$UV_VERSION (CI-pinned) ..." >&2
  python -m pip install "uv==$UV_VERSION" >&2
fi

uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock
uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock

echo "regenerated requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock — review and commit them."

content.text: The claim is clear: the gate runs `uv pip compile` against live PyPI with no `--exclude-newer` pin. Let me check the pyproject ranges and reproduce by re-resolving with the CI-pinned uv.

content.text:
Definitive reproduction. With the exact CI-pinned uv (0.11.21) and the exact CI command, the gate re-resolves to newer in-range versions: mlflow 3.13.0→3.14.0 (exactly as the finding predicted), plus arize-phoenix, certifi, mlflow-skinny/tracing, and tqdm. The `diff` returns nonzero → the CI step runs `exit 1` → the audit job fails. None of these drifts were caused by any PR touching deps; they are purely the result of upstream publishing in-range releases after the lockfile was committed.

Let me confirm there's no `--exclude-newer` or resolution-date pin anywhere in the workflow or uv config that could mitigate this.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== exclude-newer / resolution-date anywhere? ==="; grep -rnE 'exclude.newer|resolution.date|UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER' .github/ pyproject.toml uv.toml scripts/ 2>/dev/null || echo "NONE FOUND"; echo; echo "=== uv.toml exists? ==="; ls -la uv.toml 2>/dev/null || echo "no uv.toml"

content.content:
=== exclude-newer / resolution-date anywhere? ===
NONE FOUND

=== uv.toml exists? ===
no uv.toml

content.text:
Confirmed: no `--exclude-newer`, no resolution-date pin, no `uv.toml`. The uv version pin (0.11.21) only stabilizes uv's output *format*, not the *resolution target* — uv still queries live PyPI and picks the newest in-range versions at wall-clock time.

I have everything I need.

input.reasoning:
Reproduced the defect end-to-end with the exact CI-pinned tooling and command.

What I read:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml ~126-146 (audit job "Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject"): installs `uv==0.11.21`, then runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock` and `diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/...regen.lock) || { echo ...; exit 1; }` (same for requirements-core.lock and requirements-build.lock). The `diff || exit 1` makes any mismatch a hard CI failure.
- scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh: same uv pin + same compile command, no resolution pin.
- pyproject.toml: dependencies use open ranges (e.g. `mlflow>=3.1,<4`, `arize-phoenix>=17,<18`, etc.) and ~218 transitive deps are unconstrained.

What I ran (exactly mirroring CI):
1. Installed `uv==0.11.21` (verified: `uv 0.11.21 (5aa65dd7a 2026-06-11)`).
2. Ran the gate's exact compile command against live PyPI -> EXIT 0 (resolution succeeded; no offline/error artifact).
3. Ran the gate's exact header-stripped diff vs the committed requirements.lock -> diff EXIT 1 (mismatch), 48 differing lines.

What I observed:
- Committed requirements.lock pins `mlflow==3.13.0`; the fresh re-resolution produced `mlflow==3.14.0` — precisely the example the finding cited (3.13.0->3.14.0).
- Additional in-range drift: arize-phoenix 17.6.0->17.7.0, mlflow-skinny/mlflow-tracing 3.13.0->3.14.0, certifi 2026.5.20->2026.6.17, tqdm 4.68.2->4.68.3.
- None of this drift is attributable to any code/dep change in a PR — it is purely upstream packages publishing new in-range releases after the lockfile was committed. The diff -> `exit 1` means the audit job goes red on a clean checkout today, blocking merges.

Mitigation check: grepped .github/, pyproject.toml, scripts/, and looked for uv.toml — there is NO `--exclude-newer`, no resolution-date pin, no UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER, no uv.toml. The `uv==0.11.21` pin only stabilizes output format/ordering (its stated purpose, #F-031); it does NOT pin the resolution target, so uv still selects the newest in-range versions at CI wall-clock time. The reproducibility guard is itself non-reproducible.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect: a clean-checkout PR that touches nothing dependency-related will fail the audit gate the moment any in-range transitive release lands, and it is currently failing (mlflow 3.14.0). Not out-of-scope by design. The fix is to add `--exclude-newer <committed-date>` (or an equivalent resolution-date pin / uv.toml setting) to both the CI gate and regenerate_lockfiles.sh so the sync check resolves against the same point-in-time index the lockfile was built from.

content.text: Verified K-OPS-1 as a TRUE_POSITIVE. Reproduced the failure with CI-pinned uv 0.11.21 and the exact gate command: the lockfile re-resolves to mlflow 3.14.0 (committed: 3.13.0) plus 5 other in-range drifts, the gate's `diff` returns nonzero, and there is no `--exclude-newer`/resolution-date pin anywhere to prevent it.
